ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crabs as a category; no specific Singapore Chilli Crab entry exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Singapore Chilli Crab or a sufficiently close crab dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Singapore Chilli Crab is approximately 350 g, comprising one whole mud crab (shell-on) with a generous portion of chilli-tomato or black pepper sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli Crab — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists chilli crab as a distinct Singaporean dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are drawn from HPB Foodo data for chilli crab (whole dish including sauce). Where HPB Foodo does not report a micronutrient, the value is set to -1. The input record describes three variants (chilli, pepper, masala); the chilli crab variant is used as the primary reference as it is the most documented and canonical form. Pepper crab and masala crab are noted as variants in aliases and description.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed seafood dish with sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Chilli Crab. The GI is estimated at 35 based on the analogous category of mixed seafood dishes with tomato-chilli sauce, which are predominantly protein and fat with a modest carbohydrate contribution from sauce thickeners and sugar. The University of Sydney GI Database classifies similar protein-dominant seafood dishes with sauce in the low-GI range (GI 30–45). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 20.3) / 100 ≈ 7.1, using 350 g serving × 5.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 20.3 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per typical 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Singapore Chilli Crab to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce portions, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 73% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) but moderate sugar content in sauce and significant sodium load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Singapore Chilli Crab has a low estimated GI and moderate glycemic load, making it a reasonable occasional choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sugar-containing chilli-tomato sauce should be consumed in moderation, and the high sodium content warrants attention for those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and cholesterol content, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from crab
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While crab provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health, the high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol (~298 mg per serving) in Singapore Chilli Crab require portion control and infrequent consumption for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Chilli Crab (辣椒螃蟹, Ketam Cili) is one of Singapore's most iconic national dishes, recognised internationally as a defining symbol of Singaporean cuisine. The dish features whole mud crab (Scylla serrata), typically live and of substantial size, stir-fried in a wok over high heat in a rich, semi-thick sauce made from blended chillies, tomato paste, garlic, ginger, shallots, egg, soy sauce, and sugar. The sauce is savoury, mildly spicy, slightly sweet, and tangy, with a silky texture from beaten egg stirred in at the end of cooking. The crab flesh is tender, sweet, and briny, contrasting with the bold sauce. Mantou (deep-fried or steamed Chinese buns) are traditionally served alongside to soak up the sauce. Variants include Pepper Crab (black pepper or white pepper sauce, richer and more pungent) and Masala Crab (Indian-spiced preparation found in Singaporean Indian seafood restaurants). Nutritionally, chilli crab is a moderate-calorie dish (~105 kcal per 100 g) with good protein content (~10 g per 100 g), moderate fat (~4.8 g), and a relatively low carbohydrate load (~5.8 g) primarily from sauce ingredients. Sodium is notably high (~480 mg per 100 g), making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) due to its protein and fat dominance. Crab is a source of calcium, iron, and cholesterol. Clinically, this dish is best consumed occasionally and in controlled portions by individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or those monitoring sodium and cholesterol intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chilli crab?
Is chilli crab high in sodium?
What is the difference between chilli crab and pepper crab?

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes and lists chilli crab as a distinct entry. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo data for chilli crab. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement for Singapore Chilli Crab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Several micronutrients are not reported by HPB Foodo and are set to -1. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the GI estimation and the multi-variant nature of the dish (chilli/pepper/masala).
notes: 1. The input record describes the dish as 'CHILLI / PEPPER / MASALA', indicating three preparation variants. The canonical form used here is Singapore Chilli Crab (the most documented and internationally recognised variant). Pepper Crab and Masala Crab are captured as aliases and noted in the description. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo (Health Promotion Board Singapore) for chilli crab per 100 g of the whole dish including sauce. 3. Micronutrients not reported by HPB Foodo (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are set to -1 as they are not reported in the HPB Foodo source database. 4. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood dish with sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct measurement exists. 5. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Singapore Signature Crab' to the canonical English name 'Singapore Chilli Crab', with 'Singapore Signature Crab' retained as an alias. 6. is_halal is set to true as chilli crab prepared without pork or alcohol is halal; however, consumers should verify with individual restaurants. 7. Price data from the input (SGD 99.90) reflects a premium restaurant serving and is not used in nutritional calculations.
